specific_change: Downsize the primary RDS instance from db.r6g.xlarge to db.r6g.large and enable storage auto-scaling on the database to eliminate manual storage management overhead.

conclusion.headline: Downsize db.r6g.xlarge → db.r6g.large and enable storage auto-scaling; save ~$1,680/mo

telemetry_observations: db_query_p95_latency_ms p95 is 80.9ms — well under the 800ms SLA target.
db_connections_p95 peaks at 35, with a mean of 26 — far below xlarge connection limits.
db_io_wait_p95 peaks at 4.0ms with a p95 of 3.7ms, indicating negligible I/O pressure.
db_cache_hit_ratio stays above 0.956 (mean 0.974), showing the working set fits comfortably in memory.
All metrics exhibit low stddev, confirming flat, stable utilization across the full 14-day window.
infrastructure_context: Primary RDS instance is db.r6g.xlarge (4 vCPUs, 32 GiB RAM) — significantly oversized for observed load.
allocated_storage is fixed at 200 GB with no auto-scaling configured, requiring manual intervention for growth.
Single-tier topology: entire $3,360/mo cost is attributed to the database.

reasoning: Every database metric — query latency, connection count, I/O wait, and cache hit ratio — is flat and low across all 14 days, indicating the db.r6g.xlarge instance is substantially over-provisioned. A db.r6g.large (2 vCPUs, 16 GiB RAM) still provides ample headroom: peak connections (35) remain well within limits, the working set easily fits in 16 GiB given the >97% cache hit ratio, and query latency at p95 (80.9ms) leaves a 10× margin to the 800ms SLA. Enabling storage auto-scaling removes the operational burden of manual storage management at negligible additional cost.

projected_state.memory_p95_pct_estimate: ~40-55% (16 GiB still covers working set given 97%+ cache hit ratio)
projected_state.latency_p95_ms_estimate: 80-100ms (slight increase possible but well within 800ms SLA)
projected_state.notes: db.r6g.large provides 2 vCPUs and 16 GiB RAM. Given current flat-low utilization, all metrics should remain within safe operating ranges.

cost_impact.notes: db.r6g.xlarge on-demand ≈ $0.4608/hr × 730 hrs ≈ $3,360/mo. db.r6g.large ≈ $0.2304/hr × 730 hrs ≈ $1,680/mo. Halving the instance class yields ~50% savings ($1,680/mo). Storage auto-scaling adds negligible cost.

risk_assessment.primary_risk: Halving memory from 32 GiB to 16 GiB could degrade cache hit ratio if the working set grows, leading to increased I/O wait and higher query latency.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Monitor db_cache_hit_ratio, db_io_wait_p95, and db_query_p95_latency_ms closely for 2 weeks post-change. Set CloudWatch alarms at cache hit ratio < 0.95 and query p95 > 200ms to catch degradation early.
risk_assessment.rollback: Modify the RDS instance class back to db.r6g.xlarge via Terraform apply. The change requires a brief maintenance window (~5-10 min downtime) but is fully reversible.
risk_assessment.notes: Schedule the downsize during a low-traffic maintenance window. The 99.7% SLA allows ~2.2 hrs/mo downtime, so a brief resize is acceptable.
